problem_summary: 2019 MCM Problem C (data): analyze the U.S. opioid crisis across 462 counties in 5 states (OH, KY, WV, VA, TN) using DEA/NFLIS synthetic-opioid & heroin drug-identification counts (2010-2017) plus U.S. Census ACS socio-economic data (2010-2016). Tasks: (a) describe the spread/characteristics of opioid incidents and identify likely origin locations; (b) link the crisis to Census socio-economic factors; (c) use those factors to propose and test a counter-strategy. This team (1906204) framed the whole solution around a "Recommendation System" analogy — find similar geographic zones, then exploit that similarity to trace origins, predict spread, and identify high-risk counties.
models_used: K-means clustering on (longitude, latitude) with k=100 to aggregate 462 counties into ~100 'Zones' (2-8 counties each) for noise reduction

Custom inter-zone similarity metric combining 3 weighted factors: (1) Pearson/numpy.corrcoef correlation of each drug's 6-year time series, (2) geographic distance via haversine formula thresholded at 200 km, (3) drug-identification size with exponential time-decay weighting Total = sum(data_i * v^(i-1)), v=0.7

Weighted directed graph + 'Walk Around' (random-walk) origin tracing: directed edges from higher-volume to lower-volume zones weighted by similarity p(A->B); top-5 out-degree nodes form a candidate set; 100 random walks per candidate; Score_i = sum_j n_j * W_i * p(i->j); argmax = source
SVR (Support Vector Regression) for time-series forecasting of 2018-2019 drug counts, with kernel/parameter grid search (poly degree-3 chosen, LSE 28908 vs rbf best 30788)

Recommendation-augmented SVR ensemble: for each (zone, drug) build 11 SVR features — 1 from the zone itself + 10 from its most-similar zones — each feature reweighted by 1/(0.01 + difference^2) and normalized; train/held-out/test split (2010-15 / 2016 / 2017)

Two-stage SVM 'discriminator' for crisis threshold: One-Class SVM (Gaussian kernel, Schölkopf novelty detection) on unlabeled county data to auto-label outliers as 'in-crisis', then a linear SVM trained on those labels using (total opioid reports, total drug reports) axes as an alert filter

Apriori association-rule learning on Booleanized Census features (K-means binarization of HC03_VCxx percentage columns; counties as 'baskets', demographics + DRUG as 'items'); support/confidence/lift to mine demographic->opioid rules, run per-category across 16 feature groups
Pearson correlation analysis (|corr|>0.8 threshold, with autocorrelation smoothness check) to filter Apriori output down to 3 positively-correlated factors
PCA (implemented via SVD) to reduce the 3 selected factors to a single n×1 component fed into the SVR predictor

Equivalent-substitution strategy verification: model the addicted population as S * sum(alpha_i * beta_i); since per-group addiction rate beta_i is unknown, decrease group-proportion alpha_i instead (alpha'_j = alpha_j * beta'_j/beta_i) to simulate the effect of an intervention, valid only for small deltas where S ≈ S'
key_techniques: Single unifying metaphor (Recommendation System / collaborative filtering) used as a narrative spine that ties every sub-model together — clustering, origin tracing, and prediction are all reframed as 'find similar zones'
Spatial aggregation (county -> zone via geographic K-means) as a deliberate noise-reduction step to fix sparse per-drug, per-county counts before any modeling
Engineering a composite similarity score from heterogeneous signals (temporal correlation + spatial distance + magnitude with time decay) rather than using a single off-the-shelf distance
Reusing one artifact (the similarity matrix) across three different deliverables: graph edge weights for origin tracing, neighbor selection for the SVR ensemble, and zone grouping
Recommendation-style feature borrowing: predicting a zone's trajectory using SVR fits from its 10 most-similar zones, weighted by recent predictive accuracy
Two-stage SVM (unsupervised One-Class SVM to manufacture labels, then supervised linear SVM) to convert an unlabeled threshold problem into a trainable classifier
Hyperparameter selection shown as explicit grid-search tables (poly degree vs rbf gamma/C with least-square-error values) rather than asserted

Three-stage automated feature funnel for the data-heavy Census set: Apriori (relevance) -> Pearson correlation (sign/strength) -> PCA (dimensionality) — turning ~hundreds of HC03 columns into a tractable input

Clever workaround for an unidentifiable parameter: because per-group addiction rate beta cannot be estimated from the data, intervention effects are simulated by perturbing the observable group proportion alpha instead, with an explicit small-perturbation validity bound
Required-element compliance: includes the requested one/two-page Memo to a government/CDC-style audience translating model findings into policy
why_it_won: From a judge's view this paper wins on synthesis and breadth, not on any single sophisticated model. The Recommendation-System framing is a memorable, coherent 'hook' that organizes an otherwise sprawling multi-part data problem — judges reward a clear central idea they can restate in one sentence. It hits all three required tasks plus the memo, and crucially it reuses one engineered similarity object to serve origin-tracing, forecasting, and risk classification, which reads as a unified model rather than three disconnected scripts. It demonstrates genuine ML breadth (K-means, SVR, One-Class + linear SVM, Apriori, correlation, PCA, random walk) applied with stated justification and visible parameter tuning tables, signalling competence and verification discipline. The geographic-zone aggregation shows the team understood the data's sparsity pitfall and addressed it up front. The strategy section is concrete (elderly, single female householders, education) and quantitatively 'verified' via the alpha-substitution trick, giving the policy recommendations model-backed support instead of hand-waving. Honest self-criticism (the 200 km radius and time-decay factor are admitted as un-tuned) builds credibility. Rich geographic visualizations (choropleth/heatmap predictions, the transmission graph, origin maps) make findings legible at a glance.
section_highlights: Summary sheet cleanly previews all three parts and names the four headline methods as keywords (Recommendation System, Regression, PCA, Association Rule Learning), so a judge knows the whole arc in one read. The model-overview flowchart (Figure 4) maps the two parallel pipelines (NFLIS spread pipeline vs Census factor pipeline) and is an excellent navigation aid. Section 3.1.3 (Similarity Design) is the strongest technical passage — it explicitly motivates and formalizes the 3-factor similarity with equations and the v=0.7 time decay. The 'Walk Around' origin section (3.2) pairs intuition (web-browsing analogy), graph construction rules, a candidate-set degree heuristic, and a concrete scoring formula, then validates on heroin (source = zone 32). The SVR tuning tables (Figures 9-10) are a model of showing-your-work hyperparameter selection. The strategy-verification math (4.3.1) elegantly sidesteps an unidentifiable parameter. The Memo (Section 6) correctly addresses the policy audience. Full reproducible Python (K-means, haversine, networkx graph build) is in the appendix.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Origin-tracing has weak scientific validity: the directed graph orients edges purely by current report volume (high -> low), so it can only recover today's hotspots, not true historical origin; the random walk and degree heuristic add stochasticity without a causal spread model. The appendix code even picks the origin candidate with random.randint(0,3), so 'source' is partly random

K-means on raw lon/lat uses Euclidean distance on geographic coordinates (ignoring earth curvature and road/population structure) and equal-radius clusters; k=100 is justified only by 'trying many times'
Key parameters are admitted to be ad hoc: the 200 km influence radius is 'based on life experience' and the v=0.7 / time-decay factor is unjustified — the team flags both as unsolved

The One-Class SVM manufactures the 'in-crisis' labels that the linear SVM is then trained to reproduce, so the threshold classifier is somewhat circular — it learns the outlier definition it was given, not an externally validated crisis criterion

Only 7 years of data, yet the pipeline forecasts 2018-2019 and the SVR ensemble borrows neighbor trajectories; extrapolation confidence is low and no error bars / cross-validation beyond a single held-out year are reported

Apriori requires Booleanizing continuous Census data via 2-class K-means per feature, discarding magnitude information; running it per-16-categories to control combinatorics may miss cross-category interactions

Correlation (Pearson) and association rules establish association only — the paper occasionally slips toward causal language ('factors that lead to opioids') and the policy memo treats correlated demographics as intervention levers

The strategy verification reduces beta (addiction rate) effects to alpha (group proportion) perturbations under S ≈ S', which is only valid for small changes (<~2.4%/1.6%/4%) and cannot evaluate large interventions, limiting policy usefulness
Reproducibility caveats: appendix code has transcription errors/typos (combins vs combines, 'Electricity', gcf typos) and a hard-coded local save path
reusable_patterns: Pick one organizing metaphor (here: Recommendation System / collaborative filtering) and force every sub-model to express it — gives a multi-part data problem narrative unity that judges reward
For sparse geographic count data, aggregate fine units into coarser clusters (geographic K-means zones) before modeling to stabilize trends; state it as an explicit noise-reduction assumption

Build ONE reusable engineered object (a similarity / distance matrix) and spend it across multiple deliverables (graph weights, neighbor selection, grouping) instead of building a separate model per task
Compose a similarity score from heterogeneous signals — temporal correlation + spatial distance + magnitude — and weight recent data with an explicit decay factor v^(i-1)

Three-stage feature funnel for high-dimensional auxiliary data: association rules (relevance) -> correlation (sign & strength) -> PCA (dimensionality), reducing hundreds of columns to a few model-ready inputs
Two-stage SVM pattern: when no labels exist, use One-Class SVM to define outliers, then train a supervised classifier on those labels to get an interpretable decision boundary / threshold
Show hyperparameter selection as a labeled error table (kernel x parameter -> LSE) — cheap, convincing evidence of tuning and verification
Recommendation-style ensemble forecasting: predict an entity's future by borrowing fitted models from its most-similar entities, reweighted by recent predictive accuracy 1/(eps + error^2)

When a needed parameter is unidentifiable from the data (per-group rate beta), simulate intervention effects through an observable proxy (group proportion alpha) and bound validity to small perturbations

Always include the required policy memo and translate quantitative findings into concrete, audience-appropriate recommendations; pair it with geographic visualizations (choropleth forecasts, transmission graph, origin map) for at-a-glance legibility
Add a candid Model Analysis section that names un-tuned parameters and accuracy limits — honest limitations read as maturity, not weakness
